title: Wet-felted water bottle carrier
subtitle: A snug felt sleeve for a standard 750 ml water bottle, with a twisted cord shoulder strap
excerpt: Wet-felt a close-fitting sleeve for a 750 ml water bottle, with a cord strap for carrying. The resist method shapes the open top; the finished carrier insulates the bottle and protects it in a bag.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional wet-felting practice. The resist-sleeve method for cylindrical forms is a standard intermediate British wet-felting project.

glossaryTerms.definition: A felting method using hot soapy water and agitation to lock wool fibres together into a dense, stable fabric.
A waterproof template inside a wet-felted piece that shapes an opening. For this carrier, a plastic-wrapped water bottle is the resist.
Vigorous agitation of already-felted wool to shrink and densify it further, giving the finished piece its firm, snug fit.

content.text: water bottle carrier uses the bottle itself, wrapped in polythene, as the
. Roving is laid around the cylinder,

shrinks it onto the bottle's shape, and the result is a snug sleeve that fits the specific bottle once the polythene resist is removed. The open top is formed by cutting an opening at the wrist edge after fulling.

items.name: Polythene wrap or cling film to cover the bottle
Wool or thick cord for strap

content.text: Wrap the water bottle completely in cling film. This allows the wool to shrink around the bottle during fulling without felting directly onto it. The cling film is slippery, which is what you want: once fulled, the carrier should slide off the bottle.
Step 2: Lay roving around the bottle

Lay thin wisps of roving lengthways around the bottle in three even layers, turning the bottle as you go so coverage is even. Follow with two more layers laid crossways around the cylinder. The layers alternate direction for strength; three lengthways plus two crossways gives good all-round coverage.

Pour hot soapy water over the roving layers on the bottle and press firmly to saturate. Begin rubbing with the flat of your hand, rotating the bottle as you go. Rub for 10 minutes until the roving has begun to consolidate into a rough felt surface that does not shift when you lift a corner.
Step 4: Full on the bamboo mat

Lay the bottle on the bamboo mat, roll the mat around it, and roll firmly back and forth for 5 minutes. Unroll, rotate the bottle 90 degrees, re-roll and roll again. Repeat four times. The sleeve should be visibly shrinking and tightening around the bottle.
Step 5: Remove the bottle and finish

Slide the bottle out of the sleeve (unwrap the cling film first if it has not already come free). Continue hand-rolling the empty sleeve for another 5 to 10 minutes to finish the shaping and firm the walls. Rinse in cold water. To add a strap, thread a length of wool cord through two small holes punched at the top of the carrier on opposite sides, and knot each end inside the carrier to secure.

items.problem: The sleeve is too loose and slips off the bottle
items.fix: Continue fulling the empty sleeve by hand-rolling or throwing until it has shrunk further. Wet wool continues to shrink with agitation; the sleeve will tighten as you work it.

items.problem: The sleeve has a thin patch where roving barely covers the bottle
items.cause: Uneven initial roving layout or one layer missed a section
items.fix: Before fulling is complete, open the sleeve by sliding the bottle out slightly and add extra roving to the thin patch. Wet it and continue fulling.
